_metadata.title: Parpola–Mahadevan Indus Script Sign Concordance
_metadata.source_primary: Parpola, A. (1994). Deciphering the Indus Script. Cambridge University Press. Appendix (Sign List). [not digitised in machine-readable form as of 2026]
source_secondary: Mahadevan, I. (1977). The Indus Script: Texts, Concordance and Tables. Archaeological Survey of India. Category structure from Internet Archive OCR.
Yadav, N. & Vahia, M. N. (2007). Indus Script: A Study of its Sign Design. Harappa.com.
Yadav, N. et al. (2010). Statistical Analysis of the Indus Script Using n-grams. PLOS ONE 5(3):e9506.
Wells, B. (2011). Epigraphic Approaches to Indus Writing. Oxbow Books.
NFM-Indus Script font (Buriro & Kumbhar, NFMD): PUA U+E000 = Parpola sign 1.
arXiv:2604.17828 (2026): no fully validated machine-readable cross-concordance exists.

confidence_levels.high: Explicitly cited in peer-reviewed literature with both P-number and M-number
confidence_levels.medium: Inferred from well-documented categorical cluster (fish signs P059–P065, humans P191–P198)
confidence_levels.low: Sequential offset within inferred Mahadevan category; internal ordering uncertain

_metadata.usage_notes: NFM font render_indus_glyphs.py: codepoint = 0xE000 + (parpola_seq - 1). Existing data/glyphs/indus/ images were fetched from harappa.com in Mahadevan order and are correctly labeled — this crosswalk is only needed to re-render from the NFM font. HEVESY_PAIRS in cross_script_similarity.py use Mahadevan numbers directly.
_metadata.hevesy_note: Hevesy (1932) used Marshall (1931) numbers. Parpola (1994) pp. 21-23 and Guy (1990) re-expressed the pairs in post-Mahadevan notation. HEVESY_PAIRS reconstructed from Fischer (1997) pp. 35-38, Parpola (1994) pp. 21-23, and visual-category analysis of both sign inventories.

311.description: jar sign — most frequent in Indus inscriptions
311.sources: Yadav et al. 2010 (PLOS ONE); Yadav & Vahia 2007; Wells 2011
